input.target_query: alternatives to notion for engineering documentation
input.icp_segment_label: Distributed Engineering Lead at 200-500 dev shop
input.angle: How distributed engineering teams pick a Notion alternative when half the team writes in Markdown and half doesn't — a decision framework + 30-day pilot, framed around bus-factor risk rather than feature checklists.
input.audience: Engineering Directors at 200–500-person dev shops actively evaluating a Notion replacement, especially those whose teams are split between Markdown-source-of-truth and WYSIWYG factions.

structure.heading: The bus-factor problem the listicles don't mention
structure.purpose: Defuse the dominant listicle angle by reframing the decision around knowledge loss rather than features.
key_points: Listicles compare features; the actual cost of the wrong choice is tribal-knowledge loss when senior engineers leave
Quantify bus factor — one or two engineers carrying critical decisions in their heads
Anchor in a real incident postmortem framing — what does a 'we couldn't find the docs' incident actually cost?

structure.heading: The Markdown vs WYSIWYG factional split is a workflow question, not a religious one
structure.purpose: Address the content gap the SERP misses — the Markdown/WYSIWYG factional reality inside engineering orgs.
key_points: Half the team writes Markdown by reflex (engineers from dev-tools backgrounds); half wants WYSIWYG (designers, PMs, junior engineers)
Tooling that exports clean Markdown wins the long game regardless of authoring surface
Round-trip test: import a Markdown doc, edit it in the tool, export it back — diff should be trivial
Use the segment's exact phrase 'single source of truth' here verbatim

structure.heading: Decision framework: the four tests an engineering director should actually run
structure.purpose: Give a concrete framework distinct from the SERP's generic feature comparisons.
key_points: Real-time collab parity test (paired-doc concurrent edit, including conflict handling)
Markdown source-of-truth test (round-trip import/export)
API + automation test (try to drive an RFC template via CI)
Security review test (SSO + audit log for SOC 2 readiness)

structure.heading: The 30-day pilot playbook
structure.purpose: Fill the content gap: every listicle says 'we switched'; almost none provides a phased pilot script the reader can socialize to their VP.
key_points: Week 1: import 50 critical docs (most painful ones first — that's the real test)
Week 2: route RFCs through the new tool
Week 3: measure search-hit-rate vs the old baseline
Week 4: decision meeting with data, not feels

structure.heading: When NOT to migrate — three failure modes that quietly kill these projects
structure.purpose: Build trust by naming the unsexy failure modes that listicles never cover.
key_points: Tool sprawl: layering a fifth tool on top of four others guarantees split-brain
Custom-wiki temptation: maintenance cost compounds and tooling teams hate it
Half-rolled-out: split-brain across teams kills adoption regardless of tool quality
Lead with bus-factor framing — that's the content gap. Do not lead with feature comparison.
Use the ICP's verbatim phrase 'single source of truth' in either the intro or an H2
Reference the Markdown vs WYSIWYG factional split as a known problem inside engineering orgs
Avoid the word 'tribal knowledge' in titles (overused in SERP); use it in body sparingly
Cite the SERP's #2 ('Why We Left Notion') as a foil — concede their pain, then go deeper
Recommended format per SERP analysis: decision-framework + migration-playbook hybrid
sources: Internal incident postmortem (replace with the writer's real example before publish)
